nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes and rocky inshore reefs; typically found at depths of 0–20 m in exposed, high-current areas

diet.notes: Primarily a zooplankton feeder in the wild (copepods and small crustaceans). In captivity tends to accept a wide variety of foods including frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, cyclop-eeze, chopped krill, and vitamin-enriched flakes or pellets. Feeding once or twice daily is generally advised.

avoidWith: predatory species large enough to swallow them

imageCandidates.notes: Debra Baker; 1652x1316; sharp, well-lit whole-body shot clearly showing the blue-and-yellow lateral striping and dark fins on a vibrant in-situ individual (Oqea Levu, Fiji).
xpda; 1854x1238; two individuals showing the diagnostic blue body with yellow horizontal stripes, good for a schooling species; focus is slightly soft so kept as a non-recommended group shot.

summary: The Vanderbilt Chromis is a small, peaceful Pacific reef damselfish with distinctive alternating blue and yellow stripes. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly, it tends to thrive when kept in groups of three or more.
careNotes: Vanderbilt Chromis tend to thrive in groups of three or more, where they form loose aggregations and display more natural behavior. Keeping a single specimen has been reported to lead to stress and subdued coloration. They generally fare well in reef aquariums and have not been known to nip at corals or disturb invertebrates. Vitamin-enriched foods are advised to help maintain their vivid blue-and-yellow striping. The species has been reclassified under the revised genus Pycnochromis (as P. vanderbilti) in some current taxonomic treatments, though the aquarium trade and most hobby sources continue to use Chromis vanderbilti. Conspecific aggression has been reported when group sizes are too small (e.g., two individuals), so groups of five or more in larger systems tend to distribute dominance-related interactions more evenly. Water quality should be maintained consistently, as with all reef inhabitants — regular water changes and stable parameters are generally advisable.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding. Males have been documented guarding and aerating demersal egg clutches adhered to substrate. Captive breeding is uncommon in the hobby but has been reported. No widely documented captive breeding protocol is available.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6.0 cm max TL), native range (Taiwan to Hawaiian and Pitcairn islands, north to Izu Islands, south to Rowley Shoals and Lord Howe/Rapa islands), temperature range (22.3–29.3°C), and taxonomy. FishBase lists the revised genus as Pycnochromis vanderbilti; aquarium trade retains Chromis vanderbilti.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (2 in = 5.1 cm), tank size (30 gal), water parameters (72–80°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), temperament (peaceful), diet (omnivore/zooplankton), and native range (Central Pacific, Cook Islands, Fiji). Temperature max of 80°F (26.7°C) is higher than LiveAquaria's 78°F; the FishBase max of 29.3°C was also considered; averaged max used is 27.2°C. Size disagreement noted: LiveAquaria 3 in (7.6 cm) vs AquariumDomain 2 in (5.1 cm) vs FishBase 6.0 cm — all three averaged to give range min 5.0–max 7.6 cm.

fish.breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding. Males have been documented guarding demersal egg clutches. Captive breeding is uncommon and no established protocol exists in the hobby.
